agenda_briefs.title: Council to receive 2025 Homeless Count report for Vancouver
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council will consider a report and presentation on the 2025 Homeless Count for Vancouver. This is an information item only — no bylaw or regulation is being proposed. The report provides data on homelessness in the region, which may inform future policy decisions that could affect businesses, particularly those in the Hotel & Accommodation and Retail sectors.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.	2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver
(no speakers per section 7.2(d) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
Presentation
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2026 Cultural Grants for Operating & Spaces
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on proposed 2026 Cultural Grants that would provide funding for arts and culture organizations in Burnaby. The grants cover two areas: 'Operating' (helping groups run day-to-day) and 'Cultural Spaces' (helping with buildings or venues). Some parts of this proposal need at least two-thirds of all council members to vote 'yes' to pass, as required by Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter — a special rule for certain funding decisions. This is not yet approved; council will decide at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you run a Burnaby arts or culture group, watch for the council decision — this could affect your grant eligibility or funding amount.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
2.	2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces)
Recommendations A to C, E and G require at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants for Spring 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to award grants to community sport events happening in Vancouver during the Spring of 2026. This is part of the Vancouver Community Sport Event Grant program. The recommendation requires at least two-thirds of all council members to vote 'yes' to pass, as per Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter (a special rule for certain council decisions). If approved, the grants would help local organizations host sporting events in the city.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from you at this time. Watch for the council decision to see if your local sport event may be eligible for future grants.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
3.	Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake
Recommendation A requires at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby property tax distribution for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on how to divide the total property tax levy among different classes of properties (like homes, businesses, and industry) for the 2026 tax year. This decision will determine how much of the overall tax burden falls on commercial properties versus residential properties. A proposed bylaw would set the tax rates for each class. This is not yet approved — the vote will happen at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — it will affect your 2026 property tax bill.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services
agenda_briefs.source_body:
4.	2026 Property Taxation: Distribution of Property Tax Levy
4a. Budget Resolution
4b. Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2025 property tax exemptions for non-profits
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a report that recommends granting property tax exemptions for 2025 to certain non-profit organizations in Burnaby. A proposed bylaw would allow eligible groups — such as charities, community centres, and religious institutions — to pay less or no property tax on the land and buildings they own and use for their work. This is not yet approved; council will decide at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action needed from you right now. Watch for the council decision — if approved, it will affect property tax bills for eligible non-profits.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
5.	2025 Property Tax Exemptions
(no speakers per section 7.2(d) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Coastal Flood Management Policy
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed Coastal Flood Management Policy. This policy would create new rules for building and development in areas of Burnaby that are at risk of flooding from the ocean. If approved, property owners in these flood-risk zones may face new requirements for building elevations, flood-proofing, and insurance. The goal is to protect people and property from rising sea levels and storm surges. The report outlines the proposed policy but has not yet been adopted.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is in a proposed flood-risk zone and review the draft policy before the council vote.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Insurance & Finance
agenda_briefs.source_body:
6.	Coastal Flood Management Policy
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider Vancouver Heritage Foundation annual report and board reappointment
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a recommendation to reappoint members to the Vancouver Heritage Foundation Board. This recommendation requires at least two-thirds of all council members to vote in favour, as required by Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter (the city's governing law). The decision is part of approving the foundation's 2025 annual report. If passed, it would keep the board in place to continue its heritage work.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.	Vancouver Heritage Foundation Board – Annual Report 2025
Recommendation B requires at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of theVancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on public plaza alcohol sites for 2026-2029
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to choose which public plazas in Burnaby will allow drinking alcohol from 2026 to 2029. This is part of the Alcohol Consumption in Public Plazas Program. If approved, the program would let people drink alcohol in certain outdoor public spaces during specific hours and dates. The decision has not been made yet — council will discuss and vote on the proposed sites at an upcoming meeting. Business owners near potential plaza sites should pay attention, as this could affect foot traffic, noise, and local business atmosphere.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider attending or submitting feedback if your business is near a proposed plaza site.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.	Sites for 2026-2029 Alcohol Consumption in Public Plazas Program
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby seeks grant to speed up development approvals
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider applying for a provincial grant through the Local Government Development Approvals Program. If approved, the money would help the city speed up how it reviews and approves building and development projects. This is not a new rule or fee for businesses — it is a funding request to improve the city's internal processes. A decision on whether to apply is expected at the next council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action needed from you now. Watch for the council decision — if approved, it could mean faster permit times for your projects.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.	Funding Application to Local Government Development Approvals Program
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on housing rezoning rule changes
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a package of miscellaneous amendments that would change the conditions attached to various housing rezoning approvals. These proposed changes could affect what developers and property owners must do — such as providing community amenities, affordable housing units, or public benefits — before getting final approval to build. The exact details of which rezoning conditions would be altered are not yet public, but the decision could impact current and future housing projects in Burnaby. The item is on the agenda for an upcoming council meeting and has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if passed, these changes could affect the conditions on your rezoning application or future development plans.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
10.	Miscellaneous Amendments Concerning Various Housing Rezoning Conditions
(no speakers per section 7.2(b) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Text amendment to CD-1 (839) bylaw for 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed text amendment to the Comprehensive Development (CD-1) Bylaw No. 13493 for the properties at 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue. A CD-1 bylaw is a special zoning rule that applies only to a specific site. This amendment would change the rules for what can be built or done on these two properties. The decision has not been made yet — council will consider the draft bylaw at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend the public hearing to have your say on this proposed zoning change.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.	CD-1 (839) Text Amendment: 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue
(no speakers per section 7.2(b) of the Procedure By-law)
Report

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning at 4088 Granville Street
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone 4088 Granville Street to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) zone. A CD-1 zone is a custom zoning category that allows for specific uses and building designs not permitted under standard zoning rules. This proposal is on the council agenda for discussion and a potential vote. The decision has not been made yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
12.	CD-1 Rezoning: 4088 Granville Street

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning for 3803-3823 West 10th Ave and 2553 Highbury St
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning for properties at 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street. The plan would change the land use rules (called a CD-1 rezoning) to allow a new development on these sites. A draft bylaw to make this change is up for approval at the next meeting. This is a proposal only — it has not been approved yet.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning for 310 East 14th Avenue in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the property at 310 East 14th Avenue. This is a 'CD-1' rezoning, which stands for Comprehensive Development Zone — a special zoning category that allows for a specific development plan rather than following standard zoning rules. The draft bylaw would amend the city's Zoning and Development Bylaw to allow this change. The decision has not been made yet; council will consider the proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision date — this rezoning could affect nearby property values and development plans.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning at 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the properties at 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue. A 'rezoning' means changing the official rules about what can be built on that land. This is a first step — the draft bylaw is up for approval at the next council meeting. If passed, it would amend the city's Zoning and Development Bylaw, which controls land use and building types in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No immediate action is required from the public — this item is on the agenda with no speakers scheduled. Watch for the council decision.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby amenity cost charge and density bonus bylaw updates
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to consider updates to the Amenity Cost Charge bylaw and the Density Bonus and Inclusionary Zoning policies. These changes would affect how developers pay for community amenities (like parks, childcare, and community centres) when building new projects. The report also outlines required public consultation before any new rules are approved. A proposed bylaw would update how density bonuses work — these are extra building height or floor space given to developers in exchange for community benefits like affordable housing. Council will vote on whether to move forward with these changes after hearing from the public.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to comment on how new development pays for community amenities.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
16.	2026 Financing Growth Update – Required Consultation for Amenity Cost Charge By law and Bill 16 Density Bonus and Inclusionary Zoning Updates
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw to assess property for Trounce Alley & Blood Alley Square maintenance
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would charge property owners in the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square area for the 2025 maintenance costs of those public spaces. A 'local improvement project' is a way for a city to pay for upgrades or upkeep in a specific area by billing the properties that benefit directly. If approved, this bylaw would add a special assessment to the property taxes of affected landowners to cover the cost of maintaining the alley and square next year.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is in the Trounce Alley or Blood Alley Square area and watch for the council vote on this bylaw.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Food Service
agenda_briefs.source_body: 8.	A By-law to assess real property to defray 2025 maintenance costs for the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square Local Improvement Project
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw to assess property costs for East Hastings parking project
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would assess (charge) property owners along East Hastings Street to help pay for the 2025 costs of the East Hastings Street Collective Parking Project. This means some business and property owners in that area could be billed for a share of the parking project's expenses. The bylaw has not been approved yet — it is up for discussion and a vote at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend or submit comments if you own property on East Hastings Street.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Construction & Development
agenda_briefs.source_body: 7.	A By-law to assess real property to defray 2025 costs for the East Hastings Street Collective Parking Project
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Housing agreement bylaw for 320-360 West 2nd Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would create a Housing Agreement for the property at 320-360 West 2nd Avenue. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that locks in certain conditions, like how many affordable rental units must be provided and for how long. This proposal has not been approved yet — Council will consider it at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date — this proposed bylaw could affect housing supply and development in your area.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 6.	A By-law to enact a Housing Agreement for 320-360 West 2nd Avenue
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on housing agreement amendment for 450-496 Prior Street
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would amend an existing Housing Agreement for properties at 450-496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue, and 1002 Station Street. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that sets rules about how the housing is used — for example, keeping some units affordable. This amendment would change the terms of that agreement. The decision has not been made yet; council will consider the proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date — if you live or own a business near these properties, you may want to attend or submit comments.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning for 888 West 8th Avenue in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning for the property at 888 West 8th Avenue. The owner wants to change the land use rules to allow a new development. A 'CD-1' (Comprehensive Development) zone is a special zoning category that lets the city set custom rules for a specific site, instead of using the standard zoning rules. This proposal is still in the early stages — Council will consider a referral report, which is a first step to decide whether to move forward with public hearings and further review. No final decision has been made yet.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Fast-track rental housing and small hotels in new Burnaby mixed-use district
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposal to create a new 'Mixed-Use Commercial District' that would fast-track approvals for rental housing and small hotels. If approved, this new zoning category would allow developers to build rental apartments and small hotels more quickly, without needing a full rezoning process for each project. The goal is to speed up the construction of much-needed rental homes and tourist accommodations. Council will consider the referral report at an upcoming meeting before any final decision is made.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider attending the public hearing to voice your support or concerns about this proposed zoning change.
